regulator: VARA (Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority -- Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC) / CMA (Capital Markets Authority -- federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026) / DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority -- DIFC only) / FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority -- ADGM only)
instruments: VARA Broker-Dealer Services Rulebook -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/broker-dealer-services-rulebook, current version effective 19 June 2025 (19 May 2025 issuance); governs firms licensed for the Broker-Dealer Services VA Activity -- defined in VARA's own Schedule 1 (rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/schedule-1-va-activities, directly fetched) as including 'arranging orders for the purchase and sale of Virtual Assets', accepting client orders payable in fiat or virtual currency, matching transactions, dealing for the licensee's own account, and providing issuance-related services to VA creators -- through four parts: Policies & Public Disclosures, Trading & Execution Rules, Margin Trading Rules (prudential/initial/maintenance-margin and mandatory client-agreement requirements), and Licensed Distribution Services Rules

VARA Custody Services Rulebook -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/custody-services-rulebook, current version effective 19 June 2025; governs firms licensed for the Custody Services VA Activity -- defined in VARA's Schedule 1 as 'safekeeping Virtual Assets for or on behalf of another Entity' based on verified client instructions, with only providers that segregate each client's assets in separate wallets qualifying for the licence -- through Additional Board Requirements (governance/committees/remuneration disclosure), Policies/Procedures/Public Disclosures, VA Storage and Custody Rules (general requirements, segregation, wallet management), Staking-from-Custody Rules (client instructions, safekeeping, node management, risk disclosure) and Collateral Wallet Services Rules

VARA Advisory Services Rulebook -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/advisory-services-rulebook, current version effective 19 June 2025 (7 May 2025 issuance, tracing to an original 7 February 2023 version); governs firms licensed for the Advisory Services VA Activity -- defined in Schedule 1 as 'offering, providing or agreeing to provide a personal recommendation to a client' on virtual-asset transactions -- covering client-suitability assessment (client experience, investment goals, financial capacity), staff competency, verification of client-provided information, and methodology/disclosure requirements

VARA VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/va-management-and-investment-services-rulebook, current version effective 19 June 2025; governs firms licensed for the Management and Investment Services VA Activity -- defined in Schedule 1 as acting 'as an agent, or fiduciary' to manage a client's virtual assets, including investment management and staking services for proof-of-stake networks -- through client suitability, staff competency, information verification, impermissible activities, client reporting & valuation, fees/charges, marketing, management practices, receipt and transmission of orders, and risk management & due diligence rules

Federal Decree-Law No. (32) of 2025 Concerning the Capital Market Authority -- a genuinely federal instrument, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones by its own Article 5(3) and Article 2(4)(d) ('The Authority shall exercise its powers ... within the state, excluding financial free zones'); full text fetched directly from uaecma.gov.ae (assets/download/.../federal-decree-law-no-32-of-2025-en-capital-market-authority.aspx); Article 2 establishes the Capital Market Authority (CMA) as the SCA's legal successor 'in all its rights, obligations, and contracts', in force 1 January 2026 per Article 30

Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 Concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM) by its own Article 2(4)(d); full text fetched directly from uaecma.gov.ae (assets/download/.../federal-decree-law-no-33-of-2025-en-regulation-of.aspx); Article 3(1) lists the financial activities subject to CMA licensing, supervision and oversight, including (e) Brokerage, (h) Portfolio management, (n) Financial advisory services and (o) Custodian services -- the federal statutory basis for dealing, custody, portfolio-management and advisory licensing that this pillar covers; Article 39 confirms the Authority regulates virtual-asset trading and related financial activities/services 'without prejudice to any provision in the Central Bank Law', and that trading is prohibited unless the asset is on the Authority's official virtual-asset list, operated via an Authority-approved licensed platform, and itself registered with the Authority; in force 1 January 2026 per Article 85

CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (Virtual Assets Framework) -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); reported by multiple secondary legal-alert sources (Horiz Law & Co, Clyde & Co, Dentons, Mielo Group) to have been issued in early-to-mid April 2026 and to expand licensed virtual-asset activities from three to eight -- including dealing in virtual assets as principal or agent, providing and arranging custody, providing investment advice, portfolio management and arranging investment deals, alongside operating a multilateral trading facility -- under a five-module structure (General Requirements, Conduct of Business, Alternative Trading System, AML/CFT, Prudential Requirements); the CMA's own domain (uaecma.gov.ae) carries a 13 April 2026 news item on the Virtual Assets Framework, corroborating the secondary reporting's general description, but this session could not independently fetch and quote the Decision's own text (the CMA regulations-listing page, uaecma.gov.ae/en/regulations/regulations-listing, is a JavaScript-rendered search interface this pipeline's tooling could not resolve to underlying decision text) -- the exact decision number, issuance date, and activity-by-activity capital/conduct requirements for dealing, custody and advisory specifically are therefore treated as corroborated-but-not-independently-primary-verified; see open_items

DFSA GEN Module, Chapter 3A (Crypto Token regime) -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; GEN 3A.2 (Prohibitions relating to Crypto Tokens) confirmed by direct fetch of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/entiresection/25729; the DFSA's own guidance on this page states that, under GEN chapter 2, only certain Financial Services apply directly to Crypto Tokens: Dealing in Investments as Principal, Dealing in Investments as Agent, Arranging Deals in Investments, Managing Assets, Advising on Financial Products, Providing Custody, Arranging Custody, Operating a Clearing House and Operating a Multilateral Trading Facility -- the DFSA activity list that anchors this pillar's DIFC coverage; the regime came into force 1 November 2022, with updated rules in force 12 January 2026 (dates as separately confirmed in this jurisdiction's Exchanges/VATP pillar card via direct fetch of DFSA's own hosted rulebook-history page and media-release PDF)

DFSA COB Module, Section 6.13 (Client Investments and Client Crypto Tokens) and Appendix 6 (Safe Custody Provisions) -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; the DFSA Rulebook's own Conduct of Business (COB) module page, version VER51/07-26, was fetched directly from dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/cob-6-additional-rules-investment-business-and-crypto-business and confirms COB 6.13 and COB App 6 exist as the operative safe-custody framework for Crypto Tokens, cross-referenced from GEN 3A; per corroborating secondary legal-alert reporting on the DFSA's 12 January 2026 update (not independently fetched in full article-by-article text this session), a firm Providing Custody of Crypto Tokens is subject to the Client Crypto Token provisions in COB 6.13 and the Safe Custody Provisions in COB App 6, and new COB rule A 6.5.3(2) subjects Appointed (third-party) Custody Agents to a DFSA suitability assessment addressing whether the agent is itself authorised and supervised by a financial services regulator for crypto custody, and the adequacy of its key-security and storage arrangements -- flagged in open_items as not independently verified against the full COB 6.13/App 6 provision text

FSRA VA Regulated Activities (Regulation of Virtual Asset Activities in ADGM) -- under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM; per secondary reporting corroborating this jurisdiction's Exchanges (VATP) pillar's direct-fetch findings on the FSRA's Virtual Asset Framework guidance, the regulated activities extended to Virtual Assets include Dealing in Investments as Principal, Dealing in Investments as Agent, Advising on Investments or Credit, Arranging Deals in Investments, Managing Assets, Providing Custody, and Operating a Multilateral Trading Facility -- covering this pillar's dealing, custody, advisory and management scope within ADGM; FSRA's own 10 June 2025 announcement (adgm.com/media/announcements/adgm-fsra-implements-amendments-to-its-digital-asset-regulatory-framework, fetched directly this session) confirms amendments in force with immediate effect that revised the Accepted Virtual Asset acceptance process, capital and fee requirements for Authorised Persons conducting VA regulated activities, introduced a VA-specific product intervention power, and formalised prohibitions on privacy tokens and algorithmic stablecoins; the announcement's own text does not itself break out custody- or advisory-specific requirement changes, so those specifics are drawn from secondary reporting and flagged accordingly in open_items
standing_summary: The UAE has no single national licensing regime for dealing, custody or investment-advisory activity in virtual assets; four separate regulators license and supervise this activity, each within its own distinct geography, and no sentence below should be read as describing a general 'UAE rule'. First, under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC), four separate activity-based rulebooks cover this pillar, each confirmed by direct fetch of VARA's own rulebook portal (rulebooks.vara.ae) with a current version effective 19 June 2025: the Broker-Dealer Services Rulebook (covering the Broker-Dealer Services VA Activity -- order arranging/matching, own-account dealing, margin trading with prudential and client-agreement requirements, and issuance-related distribution services); the Custody Services Rulebook (covering the Custody Services VA Activity -- safekeeping virtual assets on verified client instruction, with licensing conditioned on segregating each client's assets in separate wallets, plus governance, storage/segregation, staking-from-custody and collateral-wallet rules); the Advisory Services Rulebook (covering the Advisory Services VA Activity -- personal recommendations to clients on virtual-asset transactions, with client-suitability, staff-competency and information-verification requirements); and the VA Management and Investment Services Rulebook (covering the Management and Investment Services VA Activity -- acting as agent or fiduciary to manage a client's virtual assets, including proof-of-stake staking services, with client-suitability, risk-management, reporting/valuation and impermissible-activity rules). VARA's own Schedule 1 (VA Activities), directly fetched, supplies the activity definitions cited above. Second, under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM), the statutory basis for this pillar is genuinely federal and was independently confirmed by direct fetch of the full text of two Federal Decree-Laws hosted on the CMA's own domain (uaecma.gov.ae): Federal Decree-Law No. 32 of 2025, which established the Capital Market Authority (CMA) as the Securities and Commodities Authority's (SCA) legal successor, in force 1 January 2026, with its powers exercised 'within the state, excluding financial free zones' (Article 5(3)); and Federal Decree-Law No. 33 of 2025, whose Article 3(1) lists Brokerage, Portfolio management, Financial advisory services and Custodian services among the financial activities subject to CMA licensing, supervision and oversight, and whose Article 39 confirms the Authority regulates virtual-asset trading and related financial activities and services ('without prejudice to any provision in the Central Bank Law'), with trading prohibited unless the asset is on the Authority's official virtual-asset list, traded via an Authority-approved licensed platform, and itself registered with the Authority -- also in force 1 January 2026. Multiple secondary legal-alert sources (not independently primary-verified this session against the CMA's own decision text) report that the CMA subsequently issued Decision No. 4/R.M/2026, a Virtual Assets Framework expanding licensed virtual-asset activities from three to eight -- including dealing as principal or agent, providing and arranging custody, investment advice, portfolio management and arranging investment deals -- under five modules (General Requirements, Conduct of Business, Alternative Trading System, AML/CFT, Prudential Requirements); the CMA's own domain carries a corroborating 13 April 2026 news item announcing the Virtual Assets Framework, but this session's direct-fetch attempts against the CMA's regulations-listing page returned only a client-rendered search interface, not the Decision's operative text, so the exact decision number, date and activity-by-activity requirement figures for dealing, custody and advisory remain corroborated-but-not-independently-primary-verified (see open_items). Third, under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, the DFSA's own Rulebook (fetched directly from dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com) confirms in GEN 3A that only a defined list of Financial Services apply to Crypto Tokens, including Dealing in Investments as Principal, Dealing in Investments as Agent, Arranging Deals in Investments, Managing Assets, Advising on Financial Products, Providing Custody and Arranging Custody -- directly anchoring this pillar's DIFC scope -- alongside GEN 3A.2's prohibitions on Unsuitable Crypto Tokens, Privacy Tokens, Algorithmic Tokens, and (subject to a narrow custody exception) Utility Tokens/NFTs. Custody-specific conduct sits in COB 6.13 (Client Investments and Client Crypto Tokens) and COB Appendix 6 (Safe Custody Provisions), whose existence and cross-reference from GEN 3A were confirmed by direct fetch of the DFSA Rulebook's COB module page (version VER51/07-26); this jurisdiction's Exchanges (VATP) pillar separately confirms by direct fetch that the regime came into force 1 November 2022 with updated rules in force 12 January 2026. Secondary reporting on that 2026 update (not independently fetched article-by-article this session) describes a new suitability-assessment obligation on third-party custody agents under COB rule A 6.5.3(2), addressing the agent's own regulatory authorisation for crypto custody and the adequacy of its key-security arrangements -- flagged in open_items pending direct confirmation of the full provision text. Fourth, under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, the regulated activities extended to Virtual Assets under FSRA's Virtual Asset Framework include Dealing in Investments as Principal, Dealing in Investments as Agent, Advising on Investments or Credit, Arranging Deals in Investments, Managing Assets and Providing Custody -- covering this pillar's full scope within ADGM, per secondary reporting corroborating the direct-fetch findings recorded in this jurisdiction's Exchanges (VATP) pillar card. FSRA's own 10 June 2025 announcement, fetched directly this session from adgm.com, confirms amendments in force with immediate effect covering Accepted Virtual Asset acceptance, capital/fee requirements for VA regulated activities, a new product-intervention power, and formalised privacy-token/algorithmic-stablecoin prohibitions, but the announcement's own text does not itself isolate custody- or advisory-specific changes, so those specifics rely on secondary reporting flagged in open_items. Across all four regimes, none may be read as a national UAE rule: VARA's licence does not extend to DIFC or ADGM activity or apply federally; the CMA's federal framework expressly excludes the DIFC and ADGM; DFSA authorisation is DIFC-only; FSRA authorisation is ADGM-only. This card does not cover the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE), which the pillar brief allocates to the Banking & Money and Stablecoins pillars rather than to dealing/custody/advisory licensing.

open_items: CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (the Virtual Assets Framework) is described in this card's dealing/custody/advisory coverage using secondary legal-alert sources (Horiz Law & Co, Clyde & Co, Dentons, Mielo Group), corroborated only in general terms by a 13 April 2026 CMA news item found on uaecma.gov.ae; this session's direct-fetch attempts against the CMA's own regulations-listing page (uaecma.gov.ae/en/regulations/regulations-listing) returned a client-rendered search interface rather than the Decision's operative text, so the exact decision number, issuance date, and activity-by-activity conduct/capital requirements specific to dealing, custody and advisory (as opposed to exchange/MTF activity, covered in the Exchanges/VATP pillar) have not been independently primary-verified. Should be re-fetched once a stable, server-rendered CMA decision-text URL is identified.

DFSA COB rule A 6.5.3(2)'s third-party custody-agent suitability assessment, and the 12 January 2026 update's custody-specific changes generally, are drawn from secondary legal-alert reporting corroborating a direct fetch that confirmed COB 6.13 and COB App 6 exist and are cross-referenced from GEN 3A, but this session did not independently fetch and quote the full COB 6.13/App 6 provision text article-by-article. Should be re-confirmed against the DFSA's own COB module full text before being treated as independently primary-verified.

FSRA's custody- and advisory-specific requirements under its Virtual Asset Framework (as distinct from the exchange/MTF-focused content already primary-verified in this jurisdiction's Exchanges (VATP) pillar) are drawn from secondary reporting on the FSRA's Guidance -- Regulation of Virtual Asset Activities in ADGM; this session's direct-fetch attempts against the guidance PDF (both the adgm.com-hosted copy and its assets.adgm.com redirect target) returned HTTP 403 responses, and the ADGM/Thomson Reuters-hosted rulebook page for VA Regulated Activities also returned HTTP 403. The FSRA's 10 June 2025 amendments announcement was fetched directly and confirms amendments are in force, but its own text does not itself isolate custody- or advisory-specific provisions. Should be re-fetched once the guidance PDF or rulebook page is accessible.

VARA's Broker-Dealer, Custody, Advisory and VA Management and Investment Services Rulebooks were each fetched directly from rulebooks.vara.ae and their table-of-contents structure and current effective date (19 June 2025) were confirmed, along with the VA Activity definitions from Schedule 1; however, granular figures within each rulebook -- e.g. specific margin/leverage limits in the Broker-Dealer Services Rulebook, or a precise cold-storage/segregation percentage in the Custody Services Rulebook comparable to figures verified for other jurisdictions in this pillar -- were not extracted from the full PDF text in this pass, only from the rulebook's own web table of contents. Should be re-fetched at the article level before citing a specific numeric threshold.

This pillar excludes the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE), whose Payment Token Services Regulation and banking rules are tracked in the Banking & Money and Stablecoins pillars, not here, per this pillar's brief instruction to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.
This pillar's federal AML/CFT obligations applicable to dealing, custody and advisory licensees under all four regimes are tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar, not restated here.
